coredata.dc:title: Zn-doped Cu2S quantum dots as new high-efficiency inhibitors against human insulin fibrillation based on specific electrostatic interaction with oligomers
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Shu-Lan
Yang, Qi-Qi
Liu, Xing-Yu
Jiang, Feng-Lei
Xiong, Jiaqiang
Jiang, Peng
Liu, Yi
coredata.dc:description: Inhibition of protein fibrillation process with nanomaterials is a promising strategy to combat neurodegenerative diseases. Copper-based nanomaterials have been seldom utilized in fibrillation inhibiting research due to Copper ions are generally considered as accelerators of fibrosis. Here, we proposed ultra-small Zn doped Cu2S (Zn:Cu2S) QDs as inhibitors of human insulin (HI) fibrosis. ThT, DLS, CD and TEM confirm that Zn:Cu2S QDs effectively inhibited insulin fibrosis in a dose-dependent manner with lag phase time extended (beyond 13-time by Zn:Cu2S QDs of 1 mg·mL−1), final fibril formation and the conversion from α-helix to β-sheet reduced. Additionally, thermodynamics analyzed results reveal that the HI fluorescence quenching process is static quenching dominated, and the Zn:Cu2S QDs inhibit HI fibrosis mainly through specific electrostatic interaction with oligomers. The positively charged amino acid residues of oligomers bind to the negatively charged Zn:Cu2S QDs, which prevents the self-assembly of the oligomers from growing into mature fibers to enhance the stability of the protein. Unlike free Copper ions, the as-prepared QDs show an excellent inhibition in HI fibrillation, breaking through the bottleneck of copper-based materials in inhibiting protein fibrosis and providing a potential strategy to inhibit protein fibrosis in-situ by biosynthesizing copper-based fibrosis inhibitors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Quantum dots
Human insulin
Fibrillation inhibitor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of Cu2S and Zn:Cu2S QDs
Synthesis and characterization of Zn:Cu2S QDs
Effects of Zn:Cu2S QDs on HI fibrillation
Inhibition mechanism of Zn:Cu2S QDs on HI fibrillation

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of the Zn:Cu2S QDs. (a) Absorption and fluorescence spectra of Zn:Cu2S QDs. Insets: photographs of the Cu2S (left) and Zn:Cu2S (right) QDs in aqueous solution under 365 nm irradiation. (b) TEM and HRTEM images and (c) High resolution Cu 2p XPS spectrum of Zn:Cu2S QDs. (d) PL intensity of Zn:Cu2S QDs under varies pH values (2−12). (e) Zeta potential histogram. (f) Cellular cytotoxicity of Zn:Cu2S QDs for U87 cells.

Fibrillation kinetics of HI incubated with (a) Cu2S QDs and (b) Zn:Cu2S QDs at different concentration, measured in terms of ThT intensity; insert: final ThT fluorescence intensity. (c) The ThT fluorescence intensity of HI after co-incubated with 1 mg·mL−1 Zn:Cu2S QDs at different time. (d–f) CD spectra of HI co-incubated with Zn:Cu2S QDs for different times.
TEM images of 1 mg·mL−1 HI incubated in the presence of Zn:Cu2S QDs with different concentration (the mass concentration ratio of HI to QDs varied from 1:0.1 to 1:1).

ThT fluorescence intensity of HI incubated with Zn:Cu2S QDs (1: 0.2) introduced at different time intervals: (a–e) 0, 1, 1.5, 2, and 3 h, the black arrow shows the time of QDs addition. (f) The lag time of HI fibrillation process with different time interval QDs added. (g) The HI fibrils morphology after 12 h incubation with Zn:Cu2S QDs (1: 0.2) introduced at different time intervals.

ThT fluorescence (a) and CD spectra (b) of HI fibrils incubated with the addition of fiber seeds in the absence and presence of Zn:Cu2S QDs. The mass concentration ratio of HI:seeds:Zn:Cu2S QDs is 1:0.01:1. (c, d) Morphology of HI incubated with seeds in the absence (c) or presence (d) of Zn:Cu2S QDs with a mass ratio of 1:0.01:1 for 1 h incubation (insert: morphology of fibrils seeds).

Effects of Zn:Cu2S QDs on the fluorescence of HI oligomers at (a) 298 K with different concentration of Zn:Cu2S QDs; (b) Stern-Volmer plots for Zn:Cu2S QDs with HI oligomers at different temperatures; (c) Modified-Stern-Volmer plot for Zn:Cu2S QDs with HI oligomers at different temperatures; (d) Van't Hoff plot of Zn:Cu2S QDs to HI oligomers.
Schematic illustration of the (a) synthetic procedure and (b) the Cu-based nanomaterial (Zn:Cu2S QDs) for inhibiting HI fibrillation.
Kinetic parameters of HI fibrosis growth with different concentration of Zn:Cu2S QDs incubated.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The quenching constants and thermodynamic parameters of the interactions between Zn:Cu2S QDs and HI oligomers. R is the correlation coefficient.

head.ce:title: Zn-doped Cu2S quantum dots as new high-efficiency inhibitors against human insulin fibrillation based on specific electrostatic interaction with oligomers

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: Y. Liu, Department of Chemistry, Sauvage Center for Molecular Sciences, College of Chemistry and Molecular Sciences, Wuhan University, Wuhan 430072, P. R. China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry
Sauvage Center for Molecular Sciences
College of Chemistry and Molecular Sciences
Wuhan University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Inhibition of protein fibrillation process with nanomaterials is a promising strategy to combat neurodegenerative diseases. Copper-based nanomaterials have been seldom utilized in fibrillation inhibiting research due to Copper ions are generally considered as accelerators of fibrosis. Here, we proposed ultra-small Zn doped Cu2S (Zn:Cu2S) QDs as inhibitors of human insulin (HI) fibrosis. ThT, DLS, CD and TEM confirm that Zn:Cu2S QDs effectively inhibited insulin fibrosis in a dose-dependent manner with lag phase time extended (beyond 13-time by Zn:Cu2S QDs of 1 mg·mL−1), final fibril formation and the conversion from α-helix to β-sheet reduced. Additionally, thermodynamics analyzed results reveal that the HI fluorescence quenching process is static quenching dominated, and the Zn:Cu2S QDs inhibit HI fibrosis mainly through specific electrostatic interaction with oligomers. The positively charged amino acid residues of oligomers bind to the negatively charged Zn:Cu2S QDs, which prevents the self-assembly of the oligomers from growing into mature fibers to enhance the stability of the protein. Unlike free Copper ions, the as-prepared QDs show an excellent inhibition in HI fibrillation, breaking through the bottleneck of copper-based materials in inhibiting protein fibrosis and providing a potential strategy to inhibit protein fibrosis in-situ by biosynthesizing copper-based fibrosis inhibitors.

ce:section.ce:para: Protein misfolding and aggregation/fibrillation are common features of many neurodegenerative diseases [1]. There are increasing evidences that the mechanism of neurotoxicity in the amyloidosis is similar and is the formation of the aggregated protein [2]. Type 2 diabetes (T2D), which is an increasingly alarming global health threat, is characterized by a slowly progressive deterioration of insulin secretion, as well as deposition of islet amyloid [3]. Studies suggested that the damage of insulin signals in the brain is related to neurodegenerative diseases, and the cognitive functions of some T2D patients are impaired [4]. Therefore, studying the mechanism of inhibiting fibrosis of human insulin (HI) aggregation and developing effective inhibitors can not only benefit T2D treatment, but also promote the treatment of neurodegenerative diseases such as Alzheimer's disease (AD) to a certain extent. HI is a protein hormone with 51 amino acid residues secreted by islet β cells. It has two polypeptide chains, A chain and B chain, with 21 and 30 amino acid residues, respectively. When the HI protein molecule unfolds, the α-helix structure will change to the structure of β-sheet-rich [5]. Several studies have shown that under high temperature, low pH and stirring conditions, HI is prone to aggregate to form mature fibers β-sheet rich, which provides a great strategy for studying protein amyloidosis in vitro using HI as a model [6].

According to the reported literatures, several compounds show the ability to inhibit amyloid, such as peptide [7,8], and small molecules [9–11]. However, the water solubility and biocompatibility of inhibitors, and the presence of the blood-brain barrier (BBB) increase the failure rate of these inhibitors when used in vivo or in clinical treatment [12]. Nanomaterials are gaining substantial attention in protein fibrosis due to their own unique properties, such as high specific surface area, good water solubility, good biocompatibility, and potential capacity to cross the BBB. So far, some researchers have used different kinds of nanomaterials as inhibitor to figure out the relationship between inhibitory effects and nanostructures. Yang et al. testified that the more carboxyl groups, the tighter binds of carbon dots to HI through electrostatic interaction, and the main active binding sites are located in His5 and His10, which results in better inhibition effect [12]. Liu's group studied the fibrillation properties of Human Serum Albumin (HSA) after surface charge modification, and found that the aggregation and fibrillation rate of aminated HSA was faster than that of unmodified or succinylated HSA, which means that the different surface charges of proteins can affect the interaction between QDs and proteins, and negative charged nanomaterials are good candidates as inhibitors [13]. Li's group reported that N-acetyl-L-cysteine (NAC) capped CdTe quantum dots (QDs) drastically inhibited the fibrillation of beta-amyloid (Aβ) in vitro at any time points [14]. However, the relevant mechanisms and effects of nanomaterials in protein fibrosis are still need to be investigated in-depth.

It has been demonstrated that the accumulation of amorphous Aβ deposits produces senile plaques, which contains high amounts of metal ions, primarily Zn(II), Cu(II) and Fe(III) [5,10]. And a large number of studies illustrated that copper ions could promote the fibrillation process of protein [15–18], resulting that copper-based nanomaterials are not considered as good protein fibrosis inhibitors. Recently, Cu2S QDs have attracted tremendous attention due to its near-infrared (NIR) fluorescence and chemodynamic therapy (CDT) effect [19]. Here, this work demonstrates a breakthrough result that Zn:Cu2S QDs were highly effective in blocking the process of amyloid fibrillation of HI. The NIR fluorescence Zn:Cu2S QDs have been synthesized with microwave-assistant, and the as-prepared Zn:Cu2S QDs exhibit high inhibition effect on HI fibrillation. Furthermore, the effect and mechanism of Zn doping on the inhibitory of HI fibrillation were also investigated (Scheme 1 ). The results showed that Zn:Cu2S QDs inhibited amyloid aggregation by reducing the conversion of α-helix to β-sheet structures, and quenched the fluorescence of HI in static quenching mode. What's more, the interaction type between HI oligomers and Zn:Cu2S QDs is mainly specific electrostatic interaction. To the best of knowledge, this is the first example of applying NIR fluorescence Zn:Cu2S QDs to effectively disrupt the fibrillation process. This founding breaks through the bottleneck of copper-based materials in inhibiting protein fibrosis and overcomes the limitations of Cu in application of amyloidosis. What's more, many studies have successfully microbial synthesized QDs, so it might be feasible to use metal ions (Cu, Zn) which are abundant in amyloidosis to biosynthesize Zn:Cu2S QDs for in-situ protein fibrosis inhibiting and even depolymerizing mature fibers. This is also the research that our group is trying.

All chemicals were analytically pure and used as received without further purification. Human insulin was from Yeasen Biological Technology Corporation. 1,1,1,3,3,3-Hexafluoroisopropanol (HFIP), glutathione (GSH, 98%) and thioflavin T was purchased from Sigma Aldrich, Copper nitrate (Cu(NO3)2·3H2O), Zinc chloride (ZnCl2), Thiourea, sodium hydroxide (NaOH), hydrochloric acid (HCl), and sodium chloride (NaCl) were obtained from China National Pharmaceutical Group Corporation. Water we used in experiments was purified with the Millipore system (18.2 MΩ cm−1).

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high resolution TEM (HRTEM) images were obtained on a JEOL JEM-2010FEF (UHR) electron microscope operated at 200 kV. Thermo Fisher 470 FT-IR spectrometer was employed to record Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum. The elemental composition was analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Fisher, ESCALAB 250Xi) with a monochromatic Al Kα X-ray source. X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained with a Bruka D8 Advanced X-Ray diffractometer (Bruker axs) using Cu K-alpha radiation at 0.154 nm, scanning rate is 1 degree·min−1. Absorption spectra were measured by Cary series UV–vis spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies). PL spectra were recorded on LS-55 Fluorescence Photometer (PerkinElmer) and Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies). Zeta potential was measure with Zetasizer NanoZS90 (Malvern).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Cu2S and Zn:Cu2S QDs
ce:section.ce:para: The Cu2S and Zn:Cu2S QDs were synthesized with microwave assistance. In brief, the Cu(NO3)2·3H2O aqueous solution (and Zn precursor with molar ratio 25%) and GSH were mixed evenly, and the solution was light yellow (white) and turbid. Then adjusting the pH of the mixture to 5 with NaOH, the solution became colorless and clear. After adding fresh thiourea aqueous solution, the reaction solution was transferred to a microwave reaction vessel and treated with microwave radiation. Thirty minutes later, the product Cu2S QDs (Zn:Cu2S QDs) was obtained and then purified by ultrafiltration (Millipore, 10 K), freeze-dried for further use.

The cellular viabilities were analyzed by 3-(4,5-dimethyl-2-thiazolyl)-2,5-diphenyl tetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay. The cells were planted into 96-wells plates and incubated with Dulbecco's modified Eagles' medium (DMEM) at 37 °C in a 5% CO2 atmosphere for 12 h. Then, the as-prepared QDs were added with different concentration and incubated for further 24 h. After that, MTT solution (0.5 mg·mL−1) was co-incubated with cells for 4 h at 37 °C. After discarded the solution, dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO, 150 μL) was added in each well and the plate was shaken for 20 min to dissolve the formed formazan crystals. Finally, the absorbance in 570 nm was recorded by BioTek Elx800 to indirectly reflect the number of living cells.

HI was dissolved in 1,1,1,3,3,3-hexafluoroisopropanol (HFIP) to obtain a 3 mg·ml−1 stock solution. Next, the stock solution was incubated overnight at room temperature with stirring speed of 120 rpm to separate aggregates. Finally, nitrogen was used to blow-dry HFIP, and pretreated HI was stored dried at −20 °C for further use.

The 1 mg·mL−1 HI solution was obtained by dissolving pretreated HI overnight in NaOH-HCl (pH = 2.0). To monitor the inhibiting effect of QDs on HI fibrillation process, 1 mg·mL−1 HI solution was co-incubated with QDs (mass concentration of QDs range from 0 to 1 mg·mL−1) in water bath at 60 °C. After different incubated time, aliquots were taken for further characterization.

The pre-treated protein was dissolved in HCl-NaCl buffer (100 mM, pH = 2) to obtain the 1 mg·mL−1 HI solution. Then, HI was incubated at 60 °C for 6 h, and then the fibrils mother liquor was centrifuged at 1.2 × 104 rpm for 10 min, washed three times by water. Finally, the precipitated fibrils act as seeds were dispersed in water and placed in refrigerator (−20 °C). To investigate the effect of fiber seeds on HI fibrosis and HI fibrillation inhibiting of Zn:Cu2S QDs, 0.01 mg·mL−1 seeds was co-incubated with 1 mg·mL−1 HI solution and Zn:Cu2S QDs at 60 °C.

Thioflavin T was used to monitor the kinetics of protein aggregation at room temperature whose fluorescence depends on the amount of amyloid fibrils formed. The 1 mg·mL−1 HI solution was premixed with different concentration Cu2S QDs and Zn:Cu2S QDs, respectively. And the mixture was incubated at 60 °C without stirring. Then aliquots (40 μL) were taken at different time interval. Fluorescence measurement was performed on LS-55 Fluorescence Photometer (PerkinElmer) and the fluorescence signal (excitation at 450 nm) is recorded between 460 nm and 630 nm; 5 nm slit is used for emission and excitation measurements. The 20 μL aliquot was mixed with 2 mL 50 μM ThT in PBS (pH 7.0) for fluorescence measurement.

The aliquots were further negatively stained with 2% phosphotungstic acid. The sample was washed three times with ultrapure water. Then 10 μL 100-diluted samples were dropped on a carbon-coated copper grid (icon). 3 μL of staining solution was placed on the grid and dried. Finally, the morphology of QDs and fibrils were observed with JEOL JEM-2010FEF (UHR) electron microscope.

The secondary structure content and conformational changes that occurred in HI due to the Cu2S or Zn:Cu2S QDs was characterized by circular dichroism photomultiplier (Applied Photophysics Limited, UK). At different time intervals, the sample was collected and about 20 μL of the sample was diluted 10-fold with PBS (pH = 7.0) and placed in the quartz cuvette with 0.1 cm path length. The spectra data were collected in the wavelength ranging from 190 to 260 nm at 25 °C.
Origin 9 was used for data analysis. Data are presented as mean ± standard deviation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of Zn:Cu2S QDs
ce:section.ce:para: The Cu2S QDs were prepared with microwave assistance, and the Zn:Cu2S QDs were obtained by doping Cu2S QDs with 25% Zn (see Scheme 1a and the Experimental section for detail). As shown in Figs. 1a and S1a, the fluorescence intensity was significantly enhanced without obvious change in emission wavelength (700 nm) after doping Cu2S QDs with Zn. As shown in the fluorescence photograph of Cu2S and Zn:Cu2S QDs solution, the red fluorescence of Zn:Cu2S QDs under ultraviolet excitation (λex = 365 nm) was significantly stronger than that of Cu2S QDs (Fig. 1a, insert). Changes in fluorescence lifetime of QDs usually indicate the changes in its internal properties, which may directly affect the mechanism of interaction with proteins [20–22]. The fluorescence decay results showed that the average fluorescence lifetime (τ av) of Zn:Cu2S QDs (1366.7ns) was much longer than that of Cu2S QDs (Fig. S1b), and Zn doping reduced the contribution of fast decay and changed the ratio of prompt and delayed fluorescence (Table S1), which means that Zn doping changes the energy level structures and compensates for some surface defects of Cu2S QDs [23].

TEM images show that Zn:Cu2S QDs were well dispersed and homogenous with an average size of 2.55 ± 0.07 nm (Figs. 1b and S1c). High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image shows well-resolved lattice fringe, confirming the good crystallinity of Zn:Cu2S QDs (Fig. 1b, inset). The surface analysis and chemical composition of QDs were further examined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Fourier transform-infrared (FT-IR). No absorption band of the free thiol group at 2500 cm−1 was found in the FTIR spectra of QDs (Fig. S1d), indicating that GSH has been bonded to the surface of QDs via the CuS bond [24]. The XPS spectra show that peak of element Zn appeared in the Zn:Cu2S QDs (Fig. S1e), which was consistent with the EDX results (Fig. S1f). The high-resolution spectrum of Cu fitted well into two peaks located at 951.6 eV and 931.9 eV (Fig. 1c), which corresponded to the Cu 2p1/2 and Cu 2p3/2 of Cu(I), respectively.

The acid-base tolerance abilities of QDs under broad pH ranges (pH 2–10) show that the fluorescence of Zn:Cu2S QDs was maintained well in the physiological pH range (7.35–7.45) (Fig. 1d), and it was only slightly reduced at HI incubation pH conditions (pH = 2). In contrary, Cu2S QDs were sensitive to acidic conditions, and there was no fluorescence at pH = 2, which may be due to the decomposition of QDs under strong acid conditions (Fig. S2a). The zeta potential measurement results showed that the Cu2S QDs dispersed in water show a negative surface potential (−1.68 mV), whereas obviously decreases to −26.3 mV after Zn doping at same mass concentration (Fig. 1e). Previous studies have shown that the more negative surface charge is, the better inhibitory effect of fibrosis, [13] which implied that Zn:Cu2S QDs may have greater potential to inhibit protein fibrosis compared with Cu2S QDs. Furthermore, the cytotoxicity of Cu2S and Zn:Cu2S QDs were investigated by standard MTT analysis. As shown in Figs. 1f and S2b, the survival rate of U87 cells exceeded 85% and 75% after incubated with 100 μg·mL−1 Zn:Cu2S and Cu2S QDs, respectively, for more than 24 h, indicating that the cytotoxicity of the as-prepared QDs was negligible. These results indicate that the Zn:Cu2S QDs had excellent physiological stability, feasibility of inhibiting protein fibrosis and great potential for further applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of Zn:Cu2S QDs on HI fibrillation
ce:section.ce:para:
The specific interaction between thioflavin-T (ThT) and the cross-β-fold structure of the fibrils ultimately leads to a significant increase in the fluorescence intensity of the binding complex. Their relationship can be described by the following equations (Eqs. (1)–(2)):
(1)
F =
F 0
+
F ∞
1 + exp
−
t −
t
1 / 2
τ
(2)
t lag
=
t 0
− 2 τ
where F 0 and F ∞ are ThT fluorescence intensity of HI monomer and mature fibrils, respectively. t 1/2 represents the half-time of mature fibrils obtained. F indicates the ThT fluorescence intensity after t hours incubated. t lag is the lag time of aggregation. Therefore, in this study, ThT was used as a parameter to study the effect of QDs on HI fibrosis. The results of aggregation kinetics of pure HI at 60 °C exhibited a typical sigmoidal curve, which is consists of lag stage, rapid growth phase and saturation stage, and also consistent with the previous reported results (Fig. 2a) [25]. The process of HI fibrillation was scarcely affected with Cu2S QDs co-incubated (Fig. 2a and Table S2). However, it is notable that, compared with the control group, the degree of HI fibrosis co-incubated with Zn:Cu2S QDs was significantly reduced (Fig. 2b and Table 1 ). Moreover, with the increase of introduced Zn:Cu2S QDs, the lag phase time was extremely extended from 2.84 to 39.96 h, which is more than 14 times prolonged of pure HI fibrillation process. In particular, when the mass concentration ratios of HI to Zn:Cu2S QDs increased to 1:1, the growth rate of HI fibrillation in the rapid growth phase was obvious decelerated from 2.08 to 0.36 h−1, t 1/2 prolonged to 45.46 h, and the final ThT fluorescence intensity at saturation stage was also significantly decreased (Fig. 2c). That is to say, the addition of Zn:Cu2S QDs shifted HI kinetic growth curve to right in a concentration-dependent manner, and the final ThT PL intensity was suppressed. According to reports, the process of HI fibrillation follows the typical nucleation-growth mechanism [26]. Therefore, the concentration of active monomer will directly affect the final number of mature fibrils. Based on this, it can be speculated that the effective inhibition of HI fibrosis by Zn:Cu2S QDs was probably due to the reduction in the amount of active monomer. The inhibitory effect of Zn:Cu2S QDs was further studied by analyzing the secondary structure of different stages of HI fibrosis incubated with different Zn:Cu2S QDs concentrations (Figs. S3 and 2d–f). Far UV CD spectroscopy results show that the monomer has two negative peaks at 208 and 222 nm, at the beginning of HI fibrillation process, which are attributed to the characteristic peak of α-helix. As fibrillation process continues, the two peaks of α-helix gradually disappear and a new broad peak appears at 219 nm, which corresponds to the β-sheet structure formed by partial or complete unfolding of HI, indicating the formation of the oligomers. When the fibrillation process was completed, mature fibrils was formed and the negative peak wavelength shifts to 220 nm, which represents the formation of β-sheet stacked structures (Fig. 2d). Compared with pure HI fibrosis, the changes of secondary structure with Zn:Cu2S QDs co-incubated was much slower. Moreover, as the concentration of co-incubated Zn:Cu2S QDs increased, the incubated time required longer for HI to transform from α-helix to β-sheet, which was consistent with ThT fluorescence assay.

Furthermore, the temporal evolutions of HI morphology in the process of fibrillation when co-incubation with different concentrations of Zn:Cu2S QDs were characterized with TEM (Fig. 3 ). As shown in TEM images, the HI grew from monomers to oligomers, then to protofibrils, and finally formed linear, twisted mature fibrils. The oligomers were mainly formed when incubated at 60 °C for 1 h, and a large number of fibrils formed at 4 h in the absence of Zn:Cu2S QDs co-incubated. Interestingly, the time when fibrils began to form is dependent on the concentration of co-incubated Zn:Cu2S QDs, and the thickness of mature fibrils was thinner as the concentration of Zn:Cu2S QDs increased. As shown in Fig. 3g, when the mass concentration of Zn:Cu2S QDs increased to 0.4 mg·mL−1, no fibrils were formed even when the fibrillation process was completed in the control group without Zn:Cu2S QDs. Even when the concentration of QDs is increased to the same concentration as HI (1 mg·mL−1), only a small amount of short fibers could be observed after 35 h of co-incubation, and there is no significant increase in the number of short fibers after 55 h of co-incubation. These results suggest that the Zn:Cu2S QDs greatly extended the HI nucleation period and significantly delayed the appearance of linear fibrils. TEM images show that there were a lot of QDs around the fibrils (Fig. S4a–b), and the size of Zn:Cu2S QDs was slightly increased to 3.43 ± 0.76 nm compared with its original size (2.55 ± 0.07 nm) (Fig. S4c). Reported literatures pointed out that the effective “size” of nanoparticles increases dramatically as they interacted with protein [27]. In other words, there was indeed an interaction between Zn:Cu2S QDs and HI, thereby preventing the aggregation of oligomers/fibrils to form mature fibrils. These results indicated that Zn:Cu2S QDs could significantly inhibit the lag period of HI fibrosis, and stabilize the structure of HI to a certain extent, that's the reason why the time for HI to unfold and aggregate was prolonged.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Inhibition mechanism of Zn:Cu2S QDs on HI fibrillation
ce:section.ce:para: As discussed above, during the entire fibrillation process, the HI morphology changed from monomers to spherical oligomers, then aggregates into short fibrils, and finally matures linear fibrils [28]. In order to determine which stage of the HI fibrillation process was inhibited, Zn:Cu2S QDs with mass concentration ratio of 1: 0.2 to HI was introduced during different stage of HI fibrosis. If HI monomers nucleation is inhibited, introducing inhibitors after nucleation is complete would not affect fibrosis; but if growth is inhibited, introducing inhibitors at different times during the nucleation period should have the same inhibitory effect. As shown in Fig. 4 , Zn:Cu2S QDs were introduced after 0, 1, 1.5, 2, 3 h incubation of HI, respectively. With the delay of adding Zn:Cu2S QDs from 0 to 3 h, the entire fibrosis growth time was shortened from 14 h to 6 h (Fig. 4a–e), and the nucleation time was gradually shortened from 8.6 h to 2.0 h as summarized in Fig. 4f. The earlier Zn:Cu2S QDs were added, and longer t lag was, and the more significant inhibitory effect of Zn:Cu2S QDs on the HI fibrosis lag phase. It has been reported that the HI fibrillation process is complicated, and the aggregation form is not uniform [29]. That is to say, the Zn:Cu2S QDs could inhibit the conversion of monomers into oligomers at the nucleation stage, and could inhibit the aggregation and growth of fibrils to varying degrees, but they cannot decompose mature fibrils. More interestingly, TEM images show that the later Zn:Cu2S QDs introduced, the thicker mature fibers obtained (Fig. 4g). This further suggests that the interaction between Zn: Cu2S QDs and HI monomers, Zn: Cu2S QDs and oligomers could inhibit the HI fibrillation process, but the effect is different. The same conclusion can be drawn from the relative content changes of α-helix and β-sheet in the CD spectra (Fig. S5).

Reported literatures have shown that the presence of fiber seeds would accelerate the process of HI fibrillation [30,31]. In order to clarify the main inhibition stage of Zn:Cu2S QDs on HI fibrosis, whether the inhibit effect would still maintained in the presence of fiber seeds was explored. The ThT fluorescence results show that when HI was co-incubated with fiber seeds (1% to HI) without Zn:Cu2S QDs, the lag phase of HI fibrosis disappeared, and the fibrillation process was completed within about 30 min, indicating that the fibrillation process indeed accelerated (Fig. 5a). In contrast, in the presence of Zn:Cu2S QDs, the fibrillation process was completely inhibited. Correspondingly, Zn:Cu2S QDs showed a significant impact on the secondary structure of HI, as shown in Fig. 5b. TEM images in Fig. 5c–d show the morphology of HI after 1 h co-incubation with fiber seeds. As shown in Fig. 5c insert, the seeds are short fibers. In the presence of seeds, HI monomers rapidly grew into thick and long fibrils within 1 h. When incubated with seeds and Zn:Cu2S QDs, HI monomers only grew into spherical oligomers with almost no fibers. These results indicate that the Zn:Cu2S QDs could still inhibit the self-assembly of monomer even in the presence of fiber seeds, which means that Zn:Cu2S QDs mainly inhibit the formation and growth of fibrils by interacting with oligomers.

The inherent fluorescence of proteins can be considered as an effective tool to monitor changes of protein conformation and the microenvironment surrounding of amino acid residues [32]. The fluorescence intensity of HI would decrease during the formation of insulin fibrils [33]. In order to further figure out what kind of interaction occurs between Zn:Cu2S QDs and HI oligomers to inhibit HI fibrosis, experiments of fluorescence quenching titration were conducted. The fluorescence quenching can be described by Stern-Volmer (SV) equation expressed as Eq. (3):
(3)
F 0
/ F = 1 +
K SV
Q
where F 0 and F are fluorescence intensity of HI in the absence and presence of Zn:Cu2S QDs, respectively; [Q] is the concentration of the QDs; while K SV stands for Stern-Volmer quenching constant [34]. As shown in Fig. 6a, the fluorescence intensity decreased regularly and acutely with an increasing concentration of Zn:Cu2S QDs for HI oligomers, suggesting that Zn:Cu2S QDs could interact with HI and the quenching effect was positively correlated to the concentration of QDs. Similarly, monomer fluorescence also showed the same results (Fig. S6a). To get further insight of the quenching mechanism and quenching process, the temperature dependence was judged through quenching experiments at different temperatures. For HI oligomers, it could be seen that Ksv is negatively correlated with temperature, indicating that possible complexes were formed between Zn:Cu2S QDs and oligomers, which means that the quenching type of oligomers quenched by Zn:Cu2S QDs was static quenching mechanism (Fig. 6b). However, for HI monomers, the opposite trend to titrating oligomers indicates that the type of quenching was dynamic quenching mode (Fig. S6b) [35]. The Ksv values at different temperatures of HI monomers and oligomers were listed in Tables 2 and S3. And the differences in quenching mode implies that the interaction of Zn:Cu2S QDs to HI monomers and oligomers is different.

In general, binding force between drugs and biomolecules mainly include electrostatic interactions, hydrogen bonds, van der Waals forces and hydrophobic interactions [36]. Not every interaction is an effective quenching process, so the modified Stern-Volmer equation (Lehrer equation) (Eq. (4)) was used to further correct the data [37,38]:
(4)
F 0
/ ∆ F = 1 /
f a
K a
Q
+ 1 /
f a
where f a is coefficient related to the mole fraction of the fluorophore accessible to the quencher (Fig. 6c). To figure out the interaction force between HI and Zn:Cu2S QDs, the enthalpy change (∆H) was evaluated from the Van't Hoff equation (Eq. (5)):
(5)
ln
K a
= − ∆ H / RT + ∆ S / R
where R is gas constant, K a is binding constant at corresponding temperature, and n is number of binding sites per HI. As shown in Fig. 6d, ΔH was calculated from the slope of Van't Hoff equation, free energy change (ΔG) was then calculated from the following equation (Eq. (6)):
(6)
∆ G = − RT ln
K a

And entropy change (∆S) was calculated from Eq. (7):
(7)
∆ G = ∆ H − T ∆ S

The values of ΔH, ΔS, and ΔG were summarized in Table 2. Negative ΔG indicates that the interaction between Zn:Cu2S QDs and HI oligomers occurs spontaneously. Negative ∆H and positive ∆S suggest that specific electrostatic interaction is the main force when Zn:Cu2S QDs interact with HI oligomers according to Ross's theory [34]. Due to electrostatic interaction, the positively charged amino acid residues of oligomers bind to the negatively charged Zn:Cu2S QDs, which prevents the self-assembly of oligomers from growing into mature fibrils to enhance the stability of the protein. This is the main way for Zn:Cu2S QDs to inhibit the HI fibrillation process. But for monomer, the thermodynamic parameters suggest that hydrophobic interaction between Zn:Cu2S QDs and HI monomers could occur spontaneously during HI nucleation stage (Fig. S6 and Table S3). The different interaction type between Zn:Cu2S QDs and HI in the nucleation and growth stages results in different inhibiting effects in the two stages.

In this work, we demonstrate the first application of Zn doped Cu2S QDs in inhibiting insulin amyloid fibrosis. This is an interesting result that breaks conventional thinking, because Copper ions could accelerate the process of protein fibrosis, it is believed that Copper-based nanomaterials are generally not suitable for protein fibrosis inhibitors. Fluorescence spectrometer and TEM results show that the as-prepared Zn:Cu2S QDs had a great influence on lag-phase of HI fibrosis process, significantly prolonged the time required to reach the saturation stage, and remarkably reduced the extent of final mature fibrils. CD analysis suggests that Zn:Cu2S QDs could inhibit amyloid aggregation by reducing the conversion of α-helix to β-sheet structures. Moreover, the results of the investigation of the inhibitory mechanism showed that, in addition to inhibit protein fibrosis from the monomers stage, Zn:Cu2S QDs could also restrict the self-assembly of HI to form mature fibrosis in the presence of fiber seeds. And interactions between Zn:Cu2S QDs and HI monomers, Zn: Cu2S QDs and oligomers result in the inhibition of HI fibrosis, but the effects are different. The inhibitory effect of Zn:Cu2S QDs mainly occurs in the growth stage, that is, the process of oligomers growing into mature fibers. The analysis of interaction results show that the quenching of HI oligomers fluorescence by Zn:Cu2S QDs was based on static quenching mode, and through specific electrostatic force mode, Zn:Cu2S QDs could effectively inhibit oligomers further assembly and growth into mature fibers. Therefore, the novel Zn:Cu2S QDs offer a new strategy for Cu-based nanomaterials as inhibitors of pathological protein aggregation, and provide an potential strategy to inhibit protein fibrosis in-situ by biosynthesizing Copper-based fibrosis inhibitors.

Shu-Lan Li: Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Validation, Writing - Original Draft. Qi-Qi Yang: Investigation, Methodology. Xing-Yu Liu: Investigation, Methodology. Feng-Lei Jiang: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Data curation, Validation. Jiaqiang Xiong: Investigation. Peng Jiang: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Data curation, Validation, Writing - Review & Editing. Yi Liu: Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Methodology, Writing - Review & Editing.

GuiKeAD17195081], and Bagui Scholar Program of Guangxi Province (2016).
